surviving.title: Watch is registered at the transaction's READ version, not its COMMITTED version — spurious immediate fire when the watching txn also writes the watched key
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/readpath.go:1080 (root: captures tx.readVersion instead of the post-commit committed version) — propagated via readpath.go:1209/1237 (Version: readVersion stamped into WatchValueRequest) and the eager-goroutine architecture in pkg/fdbgo/fdb/transaction.go:442-447 + pkg/fdbgo/fdb/future.go:177 (RPC sent before commit). C++ registers the watch only after commit at getCommittedVersion().
surviving.cppReference: fdbclient/NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6420 (setupWatches: watchVersion = getCommittedVersion() > 0 ? getCommittedVersion() : getReadVersion()); NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6909-6918 (commitAndWatch: commitMutations() THEN setupWatches()); ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1315/1325 (watch value = RYW read, deferred to tr.watch()); fdbserver/storageserver.actor.cpp:2626 (fire condition: reply.value != metadata->value && latest >= metadata->version) — finding said 2625, actual is 2626; storageserver.actor.cpp:13217 (waitForVersionNoTooOld(self, req.version)).
surviving.description: WatchSetup captures `readVersion := tx.readVersion` (readpath.go:1080) and threads it to buildWatchValueRequest as the WatchValueRequest.Version (readpath.go:1206-1218). libfdb_c instead registers the watch at the COMMITTED version: Transaction::setupWatches uses `getCommittedVersion() > 0 ? getCommittedVersion() : getReadVersion()` (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6420) and only runs AFTER the commit succeeds. The storage server only fires a watch when `reply.value != metadata->value && latest >= metadata->version` (storageserver.actor.cpp:2625) and waits for `req.version` before evaluating (watchValueWaitForVersion → waitForVersionNoTooOld, storageserver.actor.cpp:13217). Because Go passes the read version RV (< the commit version CV) and the futureNil goroutine starts eagerly at Watch() time (fdb/future.go:177) before Database.Transact even issues the commit, the SS evaluates the watch while latest is in [RV, CV) — i.e. before the watching txn's own write is visible — sees the OLD value (≠ the captured new value) and fires immediately. libfdb_c passes CV so the SS waits until the txn's own commit is visible, sees the matching value, and correctly stays pending until the NEXT external change.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ registers the watch post-commit at committedVersion (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6420) so the SS's `latest >= metadata->version` gate (storageserver.actor.cpp:2625) blocks until the txn's own write is durable+visible; Go uses readVersion (readpath.go:1080), letting the SS fire on the pre-commit value. Near-deterministic because Go's watch goroutine sends the RPC before Transact commits.
surviving.repro: Differential, run identical body through gofdb and cgofdb against one real FDB. (1) Seed in a SEPARATE committed txn: set k="A". (2) In ONE transaction write the watched key and watch it, then let the wrapper commit: db.Transact(func(tx){ tx.Set(k,"B"); w = tx.Watch(k); return nil,nil }). (3) Make NO external change to k afterward. (4) Assert the watch stays PENDING for a window (e.g. expectPending 300ms–1s, same helper as differential_watch_test.go). EXPECTED libfdb_c (cgo): pending — it registers at CV, SS waits to CV, reads "B" == baseline "B", no fire. ACTUAL Go: fires almost immediately — it registers at RV, SS reads pre-commit value "A" at a version in [RV,CV), "A" != baseline "B", fires. (Absent-baseline variant also works: clear k first, then {Set(k,"B"); Watch(k)} — SS reads absent != "B" pre-commit → Go fires, cgo pending.) The differential is the proof: cgo channel stays empty for the window, go channel receives nil (fired).
surviving.verifierReasoning:
Read both sides in full and the divergence is real.

GO: `WatchSetup` (readpath.go:1069-1103) captures `readVersion := tx.readVersion` at line 1080 and reads the watched value via RYW `tx.ryw.get(...)` at line 1101 — so for `Set(k,"B"); Watch(k)` the RYW read returns the txn's own write "B". `buildWatchValueRequest` (readpath.go:1206-1218) stamps `Version: readVersion` (the READ version RV) into `WatchValueRequest.Version`, and `sendWatch` (readpath.go:1221-1237) transmits it. The higher-level facade `fdb/transaction.go:429-448 Watch` runs `WatchSetup` synchronously then `newFutureNil(func(){ ... WatchPoll(...) })`; `newFutureNil` (fdb/future.go:174-182) launches the goroutine at line 177 IMMEDIATELY, i.e. inside the user's Transact body BEFORE the wrapper issues the commit. So Go sends the watch RPC at RV (< commit version CV), eagerly, racing — and beating — the multi-hop commit.

C++: `Transaction::watch` (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:5691-5699) merely pushes onto `watches` and returns the `::watch` actor (5637) which blocks on `onSetWatchTrigger`. That trigger is sent only by `setupWatches` (5586-5591 setWatch), and `commitAndWatch` (6909-6918) calls `commitMutations()` FIRST then `setupWatches()`. `setupWatches` (6420) uses `watchVersion = getCommittedVersion() > 0 ? getCommittedVersion() : getReadVersion()` — i.e. CV. The RYW layer (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1315 `watch->value = val.get()` from a read-your-writes read = "B"; deferred via `ryw->tr.watch()` at 1325) means no SS RPC is issued until after commit, at CV with value "B".

SS (the fixed server both clients hit): `watchValueWaitForVersion` (storageserver.actor.cpp:13217) does `waitForVersionNoTooOld(self, req.version)`; `watchWaitForValueChange` reads at `latest = data->version.get()` and fires iff `reply.value != metadata->value && latest >= metadata->version` (storageserver.actor.cpp:2626; the finding cited 2625 — off by one, corrected). For `seed k="A"; {Set(k,"B"); Watch(k)}`: Go's req.version=RV, value="B"; SS waits only to RV, reads value at latest in [RV,CV) → "A" (the write "B" lands at CV, not yet applied), "A" != "B" and latest>=RV → FIRES IMMEDIATELY (spurious). C++'s req.version=CV; SS waits until latest>=CV, reads "B" == baseline "B" → does NOT fire → correctly stays pending until the NEXT external change. Because the Go watch goroutine sends its single-hop RPC before/concurrent with the multi-hop commit, the SS evaluation almost always lands before CV propagates → near-deterministic spurious fire.

Both clients capture value="B" identically (Go RYW get; C++ readWithConflictRange) — the ONLY difference is the registration version (Go RV vs C++ CV) and timing (eager vs post-commit), and that alone produces the wrong observable.

Not already pinned: the differential (bench/differential_watch_test.go) and the unit tests (client/readpath_test.go TestWatch_ReadVersionSurvivesReset, correctness_test.go:1642) ALL seed/modify the watched key in a SEPARATE transaction — none has the watching txn write the watched key, so the RV-vs-CV difference never manifests (for a non-self-write watch, the value at RV equals the value at CV, so RV is harmless). This is exactly the dimensional gap CLAUDE.md warns about. TestWatch_ReadVersionSurvivesReset even documents choosing RV (codex P1) but only to dodge the postCommitReset→0 race; it captured RV where C++ uses CV, and its own NOTE concedes the SS "benignly tolerates a stale version" in the separate-txn case — precisely the case that hides this bug.

No Go mechanism avoids it: there is no post-commit re-registration at CV; the goroutine never waits for commit; AddReadConflictKey (readpath.go:1090) only affects commit-conflict, not the watch version/value.

surviving.title: rywDisabled GetKey does not clamp the resolved key to getMaxReadKey, leaking system keys (> \xff) to a normal transaction
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:959-973 (rywDisabled branch returns tx.getKey result unclamped); root no-clamp at pkg/fdbgo/client/readpath.go:223-224
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:180-185 (readThrough(GetKeyReq) clamp), dispatch at :400-401, getMaxReadKey at :2757-2762, NativeAPI-doesnt-clip comment at :172-174
surviving.description: In C++, when readYourWritesDisabled is set, GetKey is dispatched to readWithConflictRangeThrough -> readThrough(GetKeyReq) (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:400-401, 180-185), which calls the NativeAPI getKey and then CLAMPS the result: `if (ryw->getMaxReadKey() < key) return ryw->getMaxReadKey();` — filtering out system keys that the (non-readSystemKeys) transaction may not access. Go's Transaction.GetKey rywDisabled branch (transaction.go:959-963) calls tx.getKey (getKeyImpl, the NativeAPI-equivalent in readpath.go:150) and returns its result verbatim with NO clamp. getKeyImpl naturally continues resolving past the user/system boundary at \xff (its only short-circuits are allKeysEnd=\xff\xff and empty+offset<=0), so a forward selector that exhausts the user keyspace resolves into the system keyspace and the storage server returns a real \xff-prefixed key. Go returns that system key; libfdb_c returns \xff (maxReadKey). The non-snapshot rywDisabled path is the only one affected: Snapshot.GetKey (transaction.go:483) and the RYW-enabled path both route through getKeyRYW with hi=maxReadKey and clamp via readThroughEnd, matching C++.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:182-183 clamps the rywDisabled getKey result to getMaxReadKey (\xff for a normal txn); Go transaction.go:959-963 returns the raw resolved key with no clamp, so it surfaces system keys (and any key > maxReadKey) that C++ filters.
surviving.repro: Pin one read version V. On a transaction with Options().SetReadYourWritesDisable() called BEFORE any mutation (clean disable, so the storage read is not poisoned), set read version V and call GetKey with a forward selector that resolves past the entire user keyspace — e.g. FirstGreaterThan(largestUserKey), or FirstGreaterOrEqual(K) where K is just below \xff and greater than all user keys, or KeySelector{Key:"\x00", OrEqual:false, Offset: very-large} so the offset walks off the end. Differential: goGetKeyAtPinned vs cgoGetKeyAtPinned at the same V. EXPECT MISMATCH: cgo (libfdb_c) returns \xff (readThrough clamp to getMaxReadKey); Go returns a key >= \xff (a \xff-prefixed system key, or \xff\xff if the offset reaches allKeysEnd). Assert bytes.Compare(goKey, []byte{0xff}) < 0 fails while cgoKey == []byte{0xff}. Pin in differential_getkey_boundary_test.go by adding a rywDisabled variant (helper that calls SetReadYourWritesDisable before SetReadVersion) for the FGE_maxReadKey / large_pos_offset cases.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
I read both sides in full and they concretely differ.

GO (no clamp on rywDisabled GetKey):
- transaction.go:945-974 GetKey: the eager check at :951 (`bytes.Compare(selectorKey, tx.maxReadKey()) > 0` → 2004) validates ONLY the selector BASE key, not the resolved key. The rywDisabled branch at :959-960 calls `resolved, err = tx.getKey(ctx, selectorKey, orEqual, offset)` and returns `resolved` verbatim at :973 — no clamp to maxReadKey.
- readpath.go:150-236 getKeyImpl (NativeAPI-equivalent): its only short-circuits are `allKeysEnd == \xff\xff` (:170-174) and empty-key+offset≤0 (:175-177); it returns `replyKey` raw at :223-224 with NO clamp to maxReadKey. A forward selector that exhausts the user keyspace keeps looping across the \xff shard boundary into the system keyspace and returns a real \xff-prefixed key.
- transaction.go:1088-1093 maxReadKey() = []byte{0xff} for a normal (non readSystemKeys/writeSystemKeys) txn.
- The RYW-enabled and Snapshot paths instead go through ryw_getkey.go:491 getKeyRYW, which DOES clamp forward results to maxKey (readThroughEnd at :521-522, empty-window at :569). So the rywDisabled branch is the only unclamped GetKey path.

C++ (clamps): ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:397-405 readWithConflictRange dispatches readYourWritesDisabled GetKeyReq to readWithConflictRangeThrough (:354-365) → readThrough(GetKeyReq) (:180-185): `Key key = wait(ryw->tr.getKey(read.key, snapshot)); if (ryw->getMaxReadKey() < key) return ryw->getMaxReadKey();`. getMaxReadKey() (:2757-2762) = normalKeys.end = \xff for a normal txn. The comment at :172-174 ("Responsible for clipping results to the non-system keyspace ... since NativeAPI doesn't do that") is direct proof the underlying getKey returns system keys and the clamp is load-bearing.

CONCRETE DIVERGENCE on input Z: a clean txn with SetReadYourWritesDisable (set BEFORE any write, so the read is not poisoned), then GetKey of a forward selector that resolves past all user keys (e.g. FirstGreaterThan(largestUserKey), or FirstGreaterOrEqual(K) with K above all user keys, or a large positive offset). libfdb_c returns \xff (clamped by readThrough). Go returns the raw resolved key — a \xff-prefixed system key (or \xff\xff if the offset reaches allKeysEnd). Both ≥ maxReadKey, both diverge from \xff.

COVERAGE: differential_getkey_boundary_test.go (FGE_maxReadKey, large_pos_offset → \xff) uses the DEFAULT RYW-enabled path (goGetKeyAtPinned at :91-101 never calls SetReadYourWritesDisable), so it pins the getKeyRYW clamp, not this branch. differential_rywdisable_test.go GetKey cases (lines 82-97) only compare error codes on poisoned txns and resolve FGE to an already-written key (gk), so the selector never walks past the user keyspace. The rywDisabled-into-system-keys shape is unpinned. Cited goLocation (transaction.go:960) and cppReference (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:182-183) are both correct.

Severity high: this is a clear, explicitly-guarded-in-C++ wire/behavioral divergence — a Go app and a Java/C app on the same cluster get different answers for the identical GetKey, and Go surfaces system-keyspace data to a normal transaction. Trigger is narrow (RYW disabled + selector walking off the user keyspace) but the leak is a genuine correctness/compat bug.

surviving.title: too_many_watches (1032) outstanding-watch limit is never enforced; SetMaxWatches is a no-op
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/fdb/options.go:391 (SetMaxWatches no-op) and pkg/fdbgo/client/readpath.go:1069 (WatchSetup — where the increaseWatchCounter equivalent is missing); no outstanding-watch counter exists anywhere in pkg/fdbgo
surviving.cppReference: fdbclient/NativeAPI.actor.cpp:5694 (increaseWatchCounter in Transaction::watch); :2175-2180 (throw too_many_watches when outstandingWatches >= maxOutstandingWatches); :1601 + ClientKnobs.cpp:120 (DEFAULT_MAX_OUTSTANDING_WATCHES = 1e4); :2139 (max_watches option, code 20 per fdb.options:172); ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1325 (ryw->tr.watch throws if too many outstanding) — all verified accurate
surviving.description: libfdb_c caps the number of concurrently-outstanding (not-yet-fired, not-yet-cancelled) watches per Database: Transaction::watch calls cx->increaseWatchCounter() (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:5694), which throws too_many_watches (1032) once outstandingWatches >= maxOutstandingWatches (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:2175-2179), default 10000 (ClientKnobs.cpp:120) and lowerable via the max_watches database option (code 20). The throw happens synchronously at watch-registration time (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1325 `ryw->tr.watch(watch); // throws if there are too many outstanding watches`) and surfaces through the watch future. The Go client has NO outstanding-watch counter anywhere: WatchSetup (readpath.go:1069) never checks a limit and DatabaseOptions.SetMaxWatches is a no-op (fdb/options.go:391 `func (o DatabaseOptions) SetMaxWatches(_ int64) error { return nil }`). A Go app can register unlimited pending watches and never observes 1032.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ throws too_many_watches (1032) at the (maxOutstandingWatches+1)-th outstanding watch (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:2175-2179, called from :5694); Go has no counter and SetMaxWatches is ignored (options.go:391), so the error is unreachable.
surviving.repro: 1) db.Options().SetMaxWatches(1) (C: fdb_database_set_option(MAX_WATCHES, 1)). 2) tr1 = db.CreateTransaction(); w1 = tr1.Watch(k1) where k1 is never modified, so w1 stays pending → outstandingWatches=1. 3) tr2 = db.CreateTransaction(); w2 = tr2.Watch(k2) (k2 distinct, unmodified); call w2.Get() under a short bounded context. Expected: libfdb_c returns FDBError 1032 (too_many_watches) immediately at registration of the 2nd watch; Go returns no 1032 and blocks until context deadline. Differential assertion is the error code returned when registering the (max_watches+1)-th outstanding watch. NOTE: keys must stay unchanged so each watch reaches ryw->tr.watch (early-return paths at ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1308-1321 skip increaseWatchCounter if the value already changed).
surviving.verifierReasoning:
Independently verified both sides line-by-line.

C++ (libfdb_c 7.3.77) ENFORCES the cap:
- NativeAPI.actor.cpp:5694 — Transaction::watch calls trState->cx->increaseWatchCounter() synchronously at registration (line 5688: `Future<Void> Transaction::watch(Reference<Watch> watch)`, 5694: `trState->cx->increaseWatchCounter();`).
- NativeAPI.actor.cpp:2175-2180 — `void DatabaseContext::increaseWatchCounter() { if (outstandingWatches >= maxOutstandingWatches) throw too_many_watches(); ++outstandingWatches; }`.
- NativeAPI.actor.cpp:1601 — `maxOutstandingWatches = CLIENT_KNOBS->DEFAULT_MAX_OUTSTANDING_WATCHES;`; ClientKnobs.cpp:120 — `init( DEFAULT_MAX_OUTSTANDING_WATCHES, 1e4 );`.
- NativeAPI.actor.cpp:2139 — `max_watches` option sets `maxOutstandingWatches = (int)extractIntOption(value, 0, CLIENT_KNOBS->ABSOLUTE_MAX_WATCHES);` (fdb.options:172, code 20). Counter is per-DatabaseContext, decremented on fire/cancel (5679/5683, decreaseWatchCounter at 2182).
- ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1325 — `watchFuture = ryw->tr.watch(watch); // throws if there are too many outstanding watches`, caught at 1326 and rethrown, surfacing through the watch future.

Go (pkg/fdbgo) does NOT enforce it:
- fdb/options.go:391 — `func (o DatabaseOptions) SetMaxWatches(_ int64) error { return nil }` is a no-op (OPTIONS.md:112 documents "accept & ignore").
- client/readpath.go:1069 — WatchSetup only checks rywDisabled (1034) and ensureReadVersion; no watch counter. A recursive grep over all of pkg/fdbgo finds NO outstanding-watch counter/semaphore/limit of any kind; the 1032 name exists in fdb/error.go:74 but is never produced on the watch path.

Concrete differential: set max_watches=1, then register two watches on distinct unchanging keys (both stay pending). libfdb_c throws too_many_watches (1032) on the 2nd watch's future at registration time; Go's 2nd watch blocks in the long-poll with no error. Default 1e4 cap is also missing, so a runaway/leaking Go app accumulates unbounded outstanding watches where libfdb_c surfaces 1032 — both an error-code divergence and a missing resource safeguard.

Coverage: differential_watch_test.go covers fires-on-change/delete/absent-key/RYW-disabled only; grep for 1032/too_many/max_watches/outstanding returns nothing. Untested axis. Severity medium: real and trivially reachable via the option, but real-world impact is modest (default 10000 cap rarely hit; no wire/data-correctness effect).

surviving.title: OnError retry backoff is not bounded by the SetTimeout deadline; a timed-out txn still sleeps a full (growing) backoff before surfacing 1031
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1903 (OnError) — missing checkTimeout()/deadline gate at entry (should sit alongside the 1031-input check at 1916-1922) and the backoffSleep(ctx, …) calls at 1953, 1970, 1998, 2019 pass the caller ctx without bounding by tx.deadline; backoffSleep at 1886-1898 honors only ctx.Done(). Finder's location is accurate.
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1506 (entry resetPromise.isSet() throw transaction_timed_out) and :1517 (wait(resetPromise.getFuture() || tr.onError(e)) deadline race); timebomb sets+throws transaction_timed_out at :1571-1573 (creationTime+timeout). Supporting: native Transaction::onError returns delay(getBackoff()) at NativeAPI.actor.cpp:7764-7766; operator|| at flow/include/flow/genericactors.actor.h:1300 and chooseActor at :1265 propagate either-side errors. Finder's references are accurate.
surviving.description: C++ RYWImpl::onError throws transaction_timed_out immediately at entry if the timebomb already fired (:1506 `if (resetPromise.isSet()) throw …`), and otherwise caps the backoff `delay()` by racing it against resetPromise (:1517 `wait(resetPromise.getFuture() || tr.onError(e))`) so the wait aborts the moment `creationTime+timeout` passes. Go's OnError has NO deadline/checkTimeout() gate at entry — it only special-cases an INPUT error already coded 1031 (:1919). For any retryable input error it computes nextBackoff() and calls backoffSleep(ctx, d) with the caller's ctx (the Go timeout lives in tx.deadline, NOT in ctx; backoffSleep only honors ctx.Done()). So when tx.deadline has already passed (or passes mid-sleep), Go sleeps the entire backoff, then reset()s and returns nil (RETRY). The timeout is discovered only on the next operation's checkTimeout() (ensureReadVersion :614 / Commit :1445), one loop iteration later.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ caps the onError backoff at the transaction deadline (resetPromise/timebomb race, ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1517) and surfaces transaction_timed_out at ~deadline. Go ignores tx.deadline in OnError, so a SetTimeout transaction under contention overshoots its declared timeout by up to one full (growing) backoff interval — bounded by DEFAULT_MAX_BACKOFF (1s) for normal codes and RESOURCE_CONSTRAINED_MAX_BACKOFF (30s) for commit_proxy/grv_proxy memory-limit codes — and performs an extra reset/retry before returning 1031.
surviving.repro: Tightest deterministic unit repro (isolates the entry-gate half, no race timing needed): tx := &Transaction{creationTime: time.Now().Add(-1*time.Second)}; tx.SetTimeout(500) → deadline is 500ms in the PAST (already expired); assert tx.checkTimeout() != nil. Then err := tx.OnError(context.Background(), &wire.FDBError{Code: ErrNotCommitted}). Go BUG: err == nil (retry signaled) AND it first slept ~defaultBackoff. libfdb_c parity: fdb_transaction_on_error(not_committed) on an already-timed-out txn resolves the future with error 1031 immediately (RYWImpl::onError :1506). Differential assertion: Go OnError returns nil past the deadline; C returns 1031. Mid-sleep variant (the backoff-cap half): tx.SetTimeout(50); set tx.backoffJitter=func()float64{return 1} for deterministic 10→20→40→80ms backoff; call OnError(ctx, not_committed) in a loop. At the iteration where cumulative elapsed crosses 50ms, Go sleeps the full current backoff then returns nil and does one extra fn(tx) execution before the next op's checkTimeout yields 1031; libfdb_c aborts the delay at the 50ms timebomb and yields 1031 directly. Assert: total wall-clock to first 1031 exceeds deadline + one backoff, and Go executes one more body attempt than the C harness.
surviving.verifierReasoning: Read Go OnError (pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1903-2026): entry guards are only checkCancelled() (1908) and the input-already-1031 case (1919-1922) — NO checkTimeout() gate at entry. All backoff arms call backoffSleep(ctx, d) (1953/1970/1998/2019), and backoffSleep (1886-1898) honors only ctx.Done()+timer. The SetTimeout deadline lives in tx.deadline/tx.timeout (SetTimeout 2050-2063, checkTimeout 2200-2205) and is applied to RPCs only via opContext (readpath.go:95-99) — never to OnError. Transact (database.go:814-858) passes the raw parent ctx to OnError (823/851) and its top-of-loop ctx.Err() check (817) uses the parent ctx, which does not carry tx.deadline. So when the deadline has passed (or passes during the sleep), Go sleeps the full current backoff via nextBackoff (2707-2756, cap=maxBackoff/1s for normal codes, resourceConstrainedMaxBackoff/30s for 1042/1043/hot_shard/range_locked), then reset()s and returns nil (RETRY); 1031 is discovered only on the next op's checkTimeout (ensureReadVersion:614 / Commit:1445), one loop iteration + one extra fn(tx) body execution later. C++ RYWImpl::onError (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1499) throws transaction_timed_out immediately at entry if the timebomb already fired (:1506 if(resetPromise.isSet()) throw …), and otherwise caps the backoff by racing it: wait(resetPromise.getFuture() || tr.onError(e)) (:1517). Native Transaction::onError returns a pure delay(getBackoff(...)) (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:7764-7766), which is racable; operator|| (genericactors.actor.h:1300) + chooseActor (1265) propagate an error on either side, so when timebomb (:1567-1573) sets resetPromise.sendError(transaction_timed_out()) at creationTime+timeout, the || aborts the in-flight delay and onError throws 1031 at ~deadline. Concrete divergence on input Z = {SetTimeout(50ms), repeated not_committed}: at the retry where elapsed crosses 50ms, Go OnError returns nil (retry) after sleeping the full backoff and performs an extra body execution; libfdb_c on_error resolves with 1031 at ~50ms with no extra retry. No test pins this — settimeout_test.go bounds reads (RFC-112), retry_*_test.go set no timeout, and TestSetTimeout_OverallBudget (c_binding_port_test.go:1474) deliberately checks checkTimeout() directly (1503) rather than calling OnError on an expired deadline, leaving exactly this dimension unprobed.

surviving.title: Iterator() silently returns empty for Limit=-1 (ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED) and Limit<=-2, diverging from libfdb_c and from the facade's own GetSliceWithError
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/fdb/range_result.go:208 (Advance early-bail `if ri.exhausted || ri.remaining <= 0 { return false }`). Root cause spans effectiveLimit (range_result.go:64-69, only normalizes 0), Iterator() seeding remaining=effectiveLimit (range_result.go:128), and the absence in the Iterator path of the `limit < -1` -> range_limits_invalid validation that getRangeDir performs at transaction.go:1176 for the slice path.
surviving.cppReference: fdbclient/include/fdbclient/FDBTypes.h:728 (ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED = -1) and :753-756 (isValid: accepts rows==ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED, rejects rows<=-2); fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1748-1749 (!limits.isValid() -> range_limits_invalid); bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:984-991 (validate_and_update_parameters: api>13 keeps -1 as unlimited, no negative->reverse remap); cross-check cgo bindings/go/src/fdb/range.go:172-181 (Iterator keeps StreamingModeIterator) and :140-156 (GetSliceWithError forces StreamingModeExact).
surviving.description: goRangeIterator seeds remaining = effectiveLimit(options.Limit) (range_result.go:128). effectiveLimit (range_result.go:64-69) only normalizes 0 -> MaxInt32; it leaves -1 as -1 and any negative as-is. goRangeIterator.Advance() then bails at `if ri.exhausted || ri.remaining <= 0 { return false }` (line 208) BEFORE ever calling doRangeWithLimit, so a non-positive remaining yields zero rows and no error, and never even acquires a read version. For Limit=-1 this is a SILENT WRONG ANSWER: -1 is ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED (FDBTypes.h:728), valid (isValid: rows==ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED), so libfdb_c (and the pure-Go GetSliceWithError, which routes -1 through getRangeDir->getRangeImpl where remaining<=0 maps to MaxInt) returns ALL rows in the range — but the Iterator returns 0 rows. For Limit<=-2, libfdb_c returns range_limits_invalid (2012) (RYW getRange isValid check) and the facade GetSliceWithError returns 2012 via getRangeDir's `limit < -1` guard, but the Iterator returns 0 rows + nil error. The Iterator path bypasses getRangeDir's limit validation entirely. This is especially dangerous because CLAUDE.md/RangeOptions docs recommend Iterator() for large/unbounded scans, so an unbounded scan expressed as Limit=-1 over the iterator silently yields nothing.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ getRange treats rows=-1 as unlimited and returns all rows (FDBTypes.h:728,753-756; isReached() is false for -1), and rows<=-2 as range_limits_invalid (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1749-1750). Go's Iterator returns empty+nil for both, and contradicts its own GetSliceWithError on the same input.
surviving.repro: Setup: write 10 keys k0..k9 in [begin,end). (A) Internal-contradiction, no cgo needed: rrSlice := tx.GetRange(KeyRange{begin,end}, RangeOptions{Limit:-1}); rrSlice.GetSliceWithError() returns 10 rows; but it := tx.GetRange(KeyRange{begin,end}, RangeOptions{Limit:-1}).Iterator(); counting via for it.Advance() yields 0 rows, nil err -> assert both materializations agree (they must) -> FAIL. (B) Differential vs cgo for invalid limit: pure-Go tx.GetRange(KeyRange{begin,end}, RangeOptions{Limit:-2}).Iterator(); first Advance() -> false, Get() err==nil, 0 rows; cgo same shape -> first Advance() surfaces err code 2012 (range_limits_invalid) -> assert same error code -> DIVERGENCE (go=0, cgo=2012). (C) Differential vs cgo for unlimited: pure-Go Iterator(Limit:-1) yields 0 rows; cgo Iterator(Limit:-1) yields all 10 rows -> DIVERGENCE.
surviving.verifierReasoning: Read the full pure-Go iterator and both C++/cgo sides. Go: effectiveLimit (range_result.go:64-69) only normalizes 0->MaxInt32, leaving -1 as -1 and -2 as -2. Iterator() seeds remaining=effectiveLimit (range_result.go:128). Advance() (range_result.go:195-210) bails at line 208 `if ri.exhausted || ri.remaining <= 0 { return false }` BEFORE ever calling doRangeWithLimit, so for any non-positive remaining the iterator yields 0 rows + nil err and never reaches getRangeDir's `limit < -1` validation (transaction.go:1176) — no read version acquired for an ExactRange. The slice path on the SAME input behaves differently and correctly: GetSliceWithError (range_result.go:96-97) computes limit=effectiveLimit(-1)=-1, calls getRangeDir (transaction.go:1162); `-1 < -1` is false so it proceeds, and ryw.go:571-572 maps remaining<=0 to MaxInt -> returns ALL rows; for -2, getRangeDir line 1176 returns range_limits_invalid (2012). C++ spec: FDBTypes.h:728 ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED=-1; isValid (FDBTypes.h:753-756) accepts rows==ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED and rejects rows<=-2; ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:1748-1749 returns range_limits_invalid when !isValid; libfdb_c validate_and_update_parameters (bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:984-991) for api>13 keeps -1 as unlimited and does not remap. cgo RangeResult.Iterator() (bindings/go/src/fdb/range.go:172-181) keeps the original StreamingModeIterator across all batches (GetSliceWithError forces Exact), so cgo Iterator(Limit=-1) streams all rows, and Iterator(Limit=-2) surfaces error 2012 from the first batch future. Net: pure-Go Iterator(Limit=-1) returns 0 rows while libfdb_c AND pure-Go's own GetSliceWithError return all rows (both a divergence and an internal contradiction); pure-Go Iterator(Limit<=-2) returns 0 rows+nil while libfdb_c and GetSliceWithError return range_limits_invalid (2012) — the exact silent-accept-of-invalid-input class RFC-126 fixed for the slice path but missed for the iterator path. Not pinned: TestDifferential_RangeLimitInvalid (bench/differential_rangelimit_test.go:21) only exercises GetSliceWithError; range_iterator_test.go uses only positive limits.

surviving.title: rywDisabled GetKey ignores isBackward when locating the shard; a backward selector on a cross-server shard boundary loops to transaction_too_old instead of resolving
surviving.goLocation: Root cause spans three sites, all on the rywDisabled getKey path: readpath.go:179 (locate called without a backward flag) is the cited anchor and is correct; the missing infrastructure is locality.go:104 `locate`/:127 `lookupLocked` (no reverse param — forward rangeContaining only) and locality.go:570-580 `buildGetKeyServerLocationsRequest` (single-key proxy request omits the Reverse field that the range variant at :586-598 sets). Dispatch origin: transaction.go:959-960.
surviving.cppReference: NativeAPI.actor.cpp:3788 (getKey passes Reverse{k.isBackward()}) is correct. Supporting spec lines: NativeAPI.actor.cpp:1955 (getCachedLocation rangeContainingKeyBefore), :3037 (getKeyLocation_internal threads isBackward into the proxy request), :3848 (invalidateCache with Reverse{isBackward}); CommitProxyServer.actor.cpp:2704-2705 (proxy single-key rangeContainingKeyBefore on reverse); storageserver.actor.cpp:4503-4507 (SS getShardKeyRange backward branch + wrong_shard_server throw); FDBTypes.h:659/680/700.
surviving.description: C++ getKey passes Reverse{k.isBackward()} to getKeyLocation (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:3787-3788). For a backward selector (lastLessThan/lastLessOrEqual, isBackward()==true) whose key sits exactly on a shard boundary, getKeyLocation returns the shard CONTAINING THE KEY BEFORE the selector key (getCachedLocation/getKeyLocation_internal use isBackward -> rangeContainingKeyBefore, NativeAPI.actor.cpp:1955; the proxy request carries reverse=isBackward, :3037), and on wrong_shard_server it re-invalidates with Reverse{isBackward} (:2022-2029). Go's getKeyImpl locate (readpath.go:179) calls locCache.locate(key) with NO backward parameter; lookupLocked and buildGetKeyServerLocationsRequest always use rangeContaining(key) / Reverse=false (locality.go:127, 304-313). So for key K on the boundary of shard A=[P,K) and B=[K,Q), Go locates B; the storage server owning B runs getShardKeyRange with sel.isBackward() -> rangeContainingKeyBefore(K), which is not readable on B's server, and throws wrong_shard_server (storageserver.actor.cpp:4503-4507). Go invalidates K and re-locates K WITHOUT reverse -> B again -> wrong_shard again, looping until shardRetries > MaxWrongShardRetries -> ErrTransactionTooOld (readpath.go:202-209). libfdb_c locates A directly and resolves correctly.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ NativeAPI.actor.cpp:3788 + 1955 + 2022 thread isBackward through every locate/invalidate so a backward selector finds the shard ending at the key; Go's single-key locate path (readpath.go:179, locality.go) hardcodes the forward/containing shard and never passes reverse, so the SS-side isBackward shard check (storageserver.actor.cpp:4503-4507) rejects it and the client cannot recover.
surviving.repro: Stand up a multi-storage-server cluster (>=2 SS processes, configured so a shard boundary at key K splits ownership: shard A=[..,K) on SS_A and shard B=[K,..) on SS_B, e.g. via single replication + explicit shard split, so the wrong-shard bounce is non-recoverable). Write data so K is an exact shard boundary. Then: tr.Options().SetReadYourWritesDisable(); tr.GetKey(KeySelector{Key:K, OrEqual:false, Offset:0}) (lastLessThan(K), isBackward). Expected: libfdb_c routes to SS_A and returns the greatest key < K; pure-Go client routes to SS_B, gets repeated wrong_shard_server, and after MaxWrongShardRetries surfaces transaction_too_old (1007) which the Transact loop retries indefinitely (livelock). NOT reproducible on a single-process testcontainer (one SS owns both shards, so getShardKeyRange's rangeContainingKeyBefore lands on the readable shard regardless of client routing); requires a real multi-SS topology with the boundary split. Also confirm the negative control: the SAME GetKey WITHOUT SetReadYourWritesDisable resolves correctly in Go (goes through the reverse-aware getRange path), proving the rywDisabled scoping.
surviving.verifierReasoning: Read both sides in full. GO: transaction.go:959-960 — rywDisabled GetKey calls tx.getKey() directly (the RYW path at :962 goes through getKeyRYW→getRange, which IS reverse-aware via locateRange/buildGetKeyServerLocationsRangeRequest, so it is NOT affected — the finding's rywDisabled scoping is exactly right). readpath.go:179 calls locCache.locate(selectorKey) with no backward arg; locality.go:104 locate and :127 lookupLocked have no reverse parameter and use forward rangeContaining semantics (begin<=key<end), so a backward selector whose key K equals shard boundary (A=[..,K), B=[K,..)) resolves to B. On cache miss, locality.go:570-580 buildGetKeyServerLocationsRequest sets NO Reverse field (only the range variant at :586-598 does), so the proxy is asked rangeContaining(K)→B. CommitProxyServer.actor.cpp:2704-2705 confirms: single-key request returns req.reverse?rangeContainingKeyBefore:rangeContaining. storageserver.actor.cpp:4503-4507 getShardKeyRange uses rangeContainingKeyBefore(sel.getKey()) for backward selectors and throws wrong_shard_server (4507) when that range is not readable on the receiving SS — so SS_B rejects. readpath.go:202-214 then invalidate(K)+relocate→B again→loop→:204-208 shardRetries>MaxWrongShardRetries(50)→transaction_too_old (non-converging). C++: NativeAPI.actor.cpp:3788 getKey passes Reverse{k.isBackward()} to getKeyLocation; :1955 getCachedLocation uses rangeContainingKeyBefore(K)→A; :3037 getKeyLocation_internal sends GetKeyServerLocationsRequest(...,isBackward,...) so even on cache miss the proxy returns A; :3848 invalidates with Reverse{isBackward}. So libfdb_c routes to SS_A and resolves to the greatest key<K. FDBTypes.h:680 lastLessThan(k)=(k,false,0); :659 isBackward()=!orEqual&&offset<=0=true; :700 selectorInRange backward uses key<=range.end. The code-level divergence is concrete and proven. Caveat (also acknowledged by finder): observable ONLY on a multi-SS topology where the boundary K splits servers, AND only when the backward selector key equals the boundary exactly (interior backward selectors hit the same shard in both impls); a single-process testcontainer hosts both shards on one SS, so getShardKeyRange's rangeContainingKeyBefore finds the readable shard A regardless of client routing and the bug is masked. No existing differential pins it: differential_getkey_boundary_test.go uses RYW-enabled txns (reverse-aware getRange path) on a single container. Severity medium rather than high: impact-when-triggered is severe (txn never completes — transaction_too_old retried forever under stable sharding), but the trigger (rywDisabled + backward selector landing on an exact shard boundary + multi-SS split) is narrow.

surviving.title: RYW GetRange returns more=false when result exactly fills the row limit (slow path + cached fast path); libfdb_c returns more=true (limits.isReached())
surviving.goLocation: /home/birdy/projects/fdb-record-layer-go/.claude/worktrees/bughunt-2/pkg/fdbgo/client/ryw.go:615 (slow path) and :554 (cached fast path via computeMore at :730); amplified at /home/birdy/projects/fdb-record-layer-go/.claude/worktrees/bughunt-2/pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1074-1076 (rangeConflictExtent) and :1201
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:799 (result.more = result.more || limits.isReached()); GetRangeLimits::isReached() in /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/include/fdbclient/FDBTypes.h (return rows == 0 || (bytes==0 && minRows==0)); loop guard ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:685 (break only when isReached && itemsPastEnd >= 1-end.offset); end-resize :801-806 (keepItems==result.size() so more not cleared); conflict clamp addConflictRange ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:256 + :271-273 (rangeEnd = result.more ? begin : end, then keyAfter(lastKey))
surviving.description: libfdb_c's RYW getRange decrements a per-row limit and unconditionally sets `result.more = result.more || limits.isReached()`. isReached() returns rows==0, so after appending exactly `limit` rows the flag is forced true EVEN WHEN there is no further data (FDB's canonical 'limit was the stop reason' semantics — the basis for continuation-based iteration: read exactly `limit` rows and you get more=true, the next read returns 0 rows + more=false). Go instead derives `more` from whether more data actually exists. In the slow path (any pending write/clear in range) ryw.go:615 returns `take < len(batch) || serverMore || unreadableCap!=nil`; when the merged batch length == remaining limit and the server is exhausted (serverMore=false, no cap), take==len(batch) so this is FALSE -> Go returns more=false. The cached fast path (range fully in snapshotCache) at ryw.go:554 uses computeMore = `limit>0 && len(cachedKVs)>limit`, which is false when len==limit -> more=false. Only the non-cached fast path (ryw.go:564) is correct, because the wire getRangeImpl (readpath.go:752-757) already returns more=true on exactly-limit. Net: Go gets `more` right for an uncached, write-free range but wrong for a write-touched range or a cached range at the exactly-limit boundary. Result rows are identical; the `more` flag (and any continuation/cursor decision built on it) diverges from libfdb_c, which the differential harness compares directly.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:799 sets more = (storage_more) || limits.isReached(); isReached() == rows==0 (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:2857). With end=firstGreaterOrEqual and all rows < end.getKey(), the end-of-function resize keeps every row so it never clears more (keepItems==result.size()), leaving more=true. Go's ryw.go:615 / :554 compute more from residual-data presence, yielding false at the exactly-limit boundary. Existing test TestRYWGetRange_LimitWithMore (ryw_test.go:307) only covers limit < total (C,D genuinely remain) — the exactly-limit==total dimension is unprobed.
surviving.repro: New (RYW-enabled, default) transaction. tx.Set("a","1"); tx.Set("b","2") — local writes force the slow path. Then tx.GetRange(begin="a", end="z", limit=2, reverse=false) against an otherwise-empty database. Go: kvs=[a,b], more=FALSE (ryw.go:615: take=2==len(batch)=2, serverMore=false, no cap). libfdb_c: kvs=[a,b], more=TRUE (limits.rows hits 0 after the 2nd append → isReached(); loop guard at :685 breaks before the (b,z) unknown read; end-resize keeps both a,b<"z" so more stays true). Differential mismatch on `more`. Amplification: because rangeConflictExtent (transaction.go:1074) keys the read-conflict clamp off `more`, Go's wrong more=false adds the read-conflict over the FULL [a,"z") while libfdb_c clamps to [a,"b\x00"); a concurrent committed write at, e.g., "m" (in ["b\x00","z")) aborts the Go txn with 1020 not_committed but commits under libfdb_c. Cached variant (same root cause): GetRange(["a","z"), limit=0) first to fully populate snapshotCache, then GetRange(["a","z"), limit=N) with N == number of cached keys → Go fast-path-cached returns more=FALSE via computeMore (ryw.go:554/:730), libfdb_c returns more=TRUE. Reverse scans diverge identically (getRangeValueBack has the same `more || isReached()`).
surviving.verifierReasoning: VERIFIED ON BOTH SIDES. C++: I read ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp getRangeValue (the forward RYW merge). Line 799 sets `result.more = result.more || limits.isReached()`; GetRangeLimits::isReached() (FDBTypes.h) returns `rows == 0`. The is_kv branch (:776-785) calls limits.decrement() once per appended row; after appending exactly `limit` rows rows==0 → isReached()==true. The loop guard at :685 (`if (limits.isReached() && itemsPastEnd >= 1 - end.offset) break`) with end=firstGreaterOrEqual("z") (offset=1 → 1-offset=0) and itemsPastEnd=0 breaks AFTER b, before issuing the (b,z) unknown-range server read, so more is never reconsidered. The end-resize at :801-806 keeps every row (a,b < "z" so keepItems==result.size()==2) → `keepItems < result.size()` is false → more stays TRUE. So libfdb_c returns [a,b], more=true. Go: I read ryw.go getRange. The slow path is taken because hasWritesInRangeLocked is true (local sets a,b). I traced one loop iteration: fetchOrCached returns empty serverKVs+serverMore=false; mergeBatch (ryw.go:791-868, Phase-2 write inclusion) yields batch=[a,b] (boundary=nil so all in-range writes included); take=2 fills remaining=2 → the remaining<=0 return at :615 evaluates `take < len(batch)`(2<2=false) || serverMore(false) || unreadableCap!=nil(false) = FALSE. So Go returns [a,b], more=FALSE — a concrete divergence on identical rows. I confirmed `more` flows unmodified to the public API (transaction.go:505/1186 → returned directly). I confirmed the non-cached fast path is correct (readpath.go:751-757 returns more=true at exactly-limit), making the divergence asymmetric (a bug, not design). I confirmed the cached fast path (ryw.go:554) uses computeMore (:730 `len(kvs) > limit`) which is false at len==limit → also more=false, also divergent. I additionally found the divergence is amplified beyond cosmetics: rangeConflictExtent (transaction.go:1074) returns the FULL [begin,end) when more is false, vs the C++ addConflictRange clamp (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:256/:271-273) which, with result.more=true, clamps the read-conflict to keyAfter(lastReturnedKey). So Go over-registers the read-conflict tail and will spuriously abort with 1020 where libfdb_c commits. Existing coverage (TestRYWGetRange_LimitWithMore, ryw_test.go:307, and differential_getrange_conflict_test.go) only exercises limit<total where more is legitimately true; the exactly-limit==total dimension is unprobed. CONFIRMED.

surviving.title: client.Atomic accepts non-atomic / out-of-range op-codes that libfdb_c rejects with invalid_mutation_type (2018)
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1313 (Atomic — missing the op-code validity check; the commit-path validation loop at transaction.go:1503-1595 and the wire serializer at commitpath.go:346 also never validate the op byte)
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2234 (validity check), with isValidMutationType/isAtomicOp at /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/include/fdbclient/CommitTransaction.h:603-611 and ATOMIC_MASK at :572-574; CATCH_AND_DIE abort at /tmp/fdbsrc/bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1149-1150 (+137-146)
surviving.description: C++ ReadYourWritesTransaction::atomicOp validates the op-code EAGERLY: `if (!isValidMutationType(operationType) || !isAtomicOp((MutationRef::Type)operationType)) throw invalid_mutation_type();` (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2234; isValidMutationType = type<MAX_ATOMIC_OP and isAtomicOp = (ATOMIC_MASK&(1<<type))!=0, CommitTransaction.h:603/610/572). ATOMIC_MASK only contains AddValue,And,Or,Xor,AppendIfFits,Max,Min,SVK,SVV,ByteMin,ByteMax,MinV2,AndV2,CompareAndClear, so any other code (SetValue=0, ClearRange=1, DebugKeyRange=3, DebugKey=4, NoOp=5, AvailableForReuse=10, Reserved=11, or >=21) is rejected. The C binding fdb_transaction_atomic_op wraps this in CATCH_AND_DIE (bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1149), aborting the client before any write reaches the cluster. Go's Transaction.Atomic (transaction.go:1313) performs NO op-code validity check; it only does the >=510 Min/And upgrade and the SVK conflict-range special-case, then buffers Mutation{Type:op,...}. The commit path (transaction.go:1503-1595) checks metadataVersionKey, key-range, size, and versionstamp offset but never the op-code, and the wire serializer reinterprets []Mutation as []types.MutationRef verbatim (commitpath.go:344-346), shipping the raw op byte. Same CATCH_AND_DIE reasoning the project already honored for key_too_large/value_too_large (transaction.go:1538 comment 'so the oversized data never reaches the shared cluster') applies here but was missed.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ atomicOp throws invalid_mutation_type (error 2018; ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2234) and CATCH_AND_DIE aborts the client (fdb_c.cpp:1149) for any non-atomic op; Go silently buffers and commits the byte to the shared cluster.
surviving.repro:
Low-level client API on both sides (the fdb/cgofdb facades can't express a raw op-code, so the differential needs a thin raw-atomic-op shim on the C side calling C.fdb_transaction_atomic_op directly).

Destructive variant (clearest):
  Go:  tx.Set([]byte(pfx+"a"), v); tx.Set([]byte(pfx+"b"), v); commit;
       tx2.Atomic(client.MutClearRange, []byte(pfx+"a"), []byte(pfx+"c")); commit  // op-code 1
       => Go commit SUCCEEDS and the range [a,c) (incl. a,b) is DELETED.
  C:   fdb_transaction_atomic_op(tr, pfx+"a", len, pfx+"c", len, /*operation_type=*/1);
       => process aborts: "Unexpected FDB error 2018" (invalid_mutation_type) — nothing shipped.

Non-destructive variant proving the same gap:
  Go:  tx.Atomic(client.MutSetValue, k, v)  // op-code 0  => plain Set, commit OK, k==v.
  C:   fdb_transaction_atomic_op(tr, k, len, v, len, 0)  => client abort (2018).

Either way the OUTCOME diverges: Go applies/commits a mutation; libfdb_c aborts the client before any write. A differential test asserts the C call dies (or the harness expects 2018) while the Go call commits and mutates state. The fix in Go: add an isValidMutationType/isAtomicOp gate in Atomic() (or the commit loop) that rejects op-codes outside the ATOMIC_MASK set with FDBError{Code:2018}, placed after the metadataVersionKey/legal-range checks and before the size checks to match atomicOp ordering at ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2230-2237.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
I read both sides in full and the divergence is concrete and real.

GO SIDE (no op-code validity check anywhere on the atomic path):
- transaction.go:1313-1379 `func (tx *Transaction) Atomic(op MutationType, key, operand []byte)`: the ONLY op-code logic is the >=510 Min→MinV2/And→AndV2 upgrade (1319-1326) and the SetVersionstampedKey conflict-range/unreadable-range special-case (1341-1377). It then buffers `Mutation{Type: op, Key: key, Value: operand}` (1328-1332) for ANY op value. No isValidMutationType / isAtomicOp equivalent.
- transaction.go:1503-1595 (commit validation loop): I read the whole loop. It checks metadataVersionKey (1504), legal-range (1522), ClearRange end-key (1529), key size (1561), value size (1565), and versionstamp offset (1583-1594). There is NO op-code validity check. For op==MutClearRange(1) the loop hits the `if m.Type == MutClearRange` arm (1526) and treats it as a real range clear, then `continue`.
- commitpath.go:346 zero-copy reinterprets `[]Mutation` as `[]types.MutationRef`, so the raw op byte (e.g. 1=ClearRange, 0=SetValue, 99=unknown) ships verbatim to the cluster.
- Go MutationType constants (transaction.go:133-148) are 1:1 with the C++ enum (SetValue=0, ClearRange=1, AddValue=2, ...), and `MutClearRange`/`MutSetValue` are exported, valid `MutationType` values that `Atomic` accepts with no type-level or runtime barrier.

C++ SIDE (eager validity check + die):
- ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2234 `if (!isValidMutationType(operationType) || !isAtomicOp((MutationRef::Type)operationType)) throw invalid_mutation_type();` — runs after the metadataVersionKey/legal-range checks (2226-2232) and before size checks (2237).
- CommitTransaction.h:603-604 isValidMutationType = type<MAX_ATOMIC_OP; :610-611 isAtomicOp = (ATOMIC_MASK & (1<<type))!=0; :572-574 ATOMIC_MASK contains only AddValue,And,Or,Xor,AppendIfFits,Max,Min,SetVersionstampedKey,SetVersionstampedValue,ByteMin,ByteMax,MinV2,AndV2,CompareAndClear. So SetValue(0), ClearRange(1), DebugKeyRange(3), DebugKey(4), NoOp(5), etc. and any code >=MAX_ATOMIC_OP are rejected.
- bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1149-1150 `fdb_transaction_atomic_op` wraps `TXN(tr)->atomicOp(...)` in CATCH_AND_DIE (fdb_c.cpp:137-146), so the thrown invalid_mutation_type (error 2018) prints "Unexpected FDB error 2018" and abort()s the whole client process before any mutation is shipped.

CONCRETE DIFFERENCE: For X = atomicOp with op-code 1 (ClearRange), Go buffers and commits a ClearRange that DELETES [begin,end) on the shared cluster; libfdb_c aborts the client process. For op-code 0 (SetValue), Go performs a plain Set; libfdb_c aborts. This is not "garbage in / garbage out": the Go Atomic() deliberately ports RYW::atomicOp's other validations (metadataVersionKey, legal-range, size, versionstamp-offset, Min→MinV2) with comments citing exact atomicOp line numbers, but omits the :2234 op-code validity check — an identifiable gap in the port.

REACHABILITY / SEVERITY: The high-level fdb facade (fdb/transaction.go:230-309) hardcodes a valid op-code per named method, and cmd/fdb-stacktester atomicOpType (operations.go:722-750) maps only valid atomic ops (panics otherwise), so no current caller passes a bad code — hence medium, not high. But the low-level `client.Transaction.Atomic` is the direct analog of `fdb_transaction_atomic_op` and concretely diverges; the destructive ClearRange/SetValue sub-cases are the sharp edge.

COVERAGE CHECK: 2018 appears only in fdb/error.go:220 (string table) and error_test.go:50 (not-retryable assertion); no code ever PRODUCES 2018. differential_atomic_test.go covers only valid atomic ops (fold widths, ByteMin/Max, CompareAndClear, AppendIfFits, Min/AndV2 — :212-374). differential_errorcode_test.go (:43) drives the high-level facade (Set/Get) which cannot express a raw op-code, and has no invalid_mutation_type case. So this exact shape is unpinned.

surviving.title: commitDummyTransaction retry uses ±10% jitter (U[0.9,1.1)) instead of C++ getBackoff's U[0,1) of the current backoff
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/commitpath.go:186 (backoffSleep(ctx, jitterBackoff(backoff))) inside the dummy's manual retry loop (commitpath.go:142-195); jitterBackoff defined at commitpath.go:206-213 (factor = 0.9 + 0.2*rand.Float64()). Cited locations are accurate.
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6109 (returnedBackoff *= deterministicRandom()->random01()) inside Transaction::getBackoff (def at :6087). Reached from commitDummyTransaction (:6306) via wait(tr.onError(e)) (~:6340) → Transaction::onError (:7732) → getBackoff call (:7764). Initial/growth/cap constants in ClientKnobs.cpp:67-69 (DEFAULT_BACKOFF .01, DEFAULT_MAX_BACKOFF 1.0, BACKOFF_GROWTH_RATE 2.0).
surviving.description: C++ commitDummyTransaction retries via tr.onError(e) → getBackoff, whose delay is `backoff * random01()` (uniform on [0,1) of the CURRENT backoff, which itself grows 10ms→…→1s). Go's commitDummyTransaction runs its own manual loop and sleeps jitterBackoff(backoff) = backoff * (0.9 + 0.2*rand) = backoff * U[0.9,1.1). So the dummy's first-retry delay is ~[9,11)ms in Go vs [0,10)ms in C++, and in general Go centers the delay at the full backoff while C++ spreads it over [0,backoff). The doubling/cap (10ms→1s) matches; only the per-attempt jitter distribution differs. The MAIN OnError path (nextBackoff, transaction.go:2712 rand.Float64()) correctly uses U[0,1), matching C++ — this divergence is confined to the internal dummy barrier loop.
surviving.whyWrong: libfdb_c's getBackoff multiplies by random01() (U[0,1)); the Go dummy multiplies by U[0.9,1.1). Same constants, different jitter law. Impact is limited to the timing of an internal commit_unknown_result synchronization barrier (no wire/data effect, no correctness effect), so severity is low.
surviving.repro: Differential focus: jitter distribution of the dummy-barrier retry sleep. Inject a retryable error (e.g. not_committed/1020) into the dummy commit on the first iteration with a fixed RNG seed (or via the backoffJitter test hook analog for the dummy loop), and compare the realized sleep against libfdb_c's getBackoff. With base backoff = 10ms: Go yields a sleep in [9ms,11ms) (10ms * U[0.9,1.1)); libfdb_c yields a delay in [0ms,10ms) (10ms * U[0,1)). Sampling N attempts, Go's first-attempt delays cluster around 10ms with min ~9ms, while libfdb_c's range down to ~0ms with mean ~5ms — a directly observable distribution divergence. (Timing-only; no key/value/wire assertion involved.)
surviving.verifierReasoning:
I independently confirmed the concrete behavioral difference by reading both sides in full.

GO (the dummy's own hand-rolled retry loop): commitpath.go:142-195 does NOT route through OnError. On a retryable dummy.Commit error it sleeps `jitterBackoff(backoff)` (commitpath.go:186). jitterBackoff is defined at commitpath.go:206-213: `factor := 0.9 + 0.2*rand.Float64(); return d*factor` → d * U[0.9,1.1). The backoff seed/curve is commitpath.go:140 `backoff := defaultBackoff` (10ms) and commitpath.go:189 `backoff = min(backoff*2, maxBackoff)` (cap 1s). So first dummy retry sleep = 10ms*U[0.9,1.1) = U[9,11)ms.

C++: commitDummyTransaction (NativeAPI.actor.cpp:6306) catches the error and does `wait(tr.onError(e));` (~:6340). Transaction::onError (:7732) for the retryable codes (not_committed/commit_unknown_result/...) runs `double backoff = getBackoff(e.code()); reset(); return delay(backoff,...)` (:7764). getBackoff (:6087) sets `returnedBackoff = backoff`, then at :6109 `returnedBackoff *= deterministicRandom()->random01();` → backoff * U[0,1), then grows backoff = min(backoff*BACKOFF_GROWTH_RATE, maxBackoff). Initial backoff = DEFAULT_BACKOFF = 0.01s, growth 2.0, cap 1.0 (ClientKnobs.cpp:67-68). So first dummy retry delay = 10ms*U[0,1) = U[0,10)ms.

Concrete divergence given a retryable dummy error on iteration 1 with deterministic RNG: Go sleeps ~U[9,11)ms (mean 10ms); libfdb_c delays ~U[0,10)ms (mean 5ms). In general Go centers each attempt's delay at the full current backoff (±10%) while C++ spreads it uniformly over [0,backoff). The exponential doubling and 1s cap match; only the per-attempt jitter law differs.

Framing claim verified: the MAIN OnError path nextBackoff (transaction.go:2707-2716) uses `jitter := rand.Float64()` (U[0,1)) then `delay := backoff*jitter` — this matches C++ getBackoff exactly. So the divergence is genuinely confined to the dummy barrier's separate manual loop; Go is even internally inconsistent (main path U[0,1), dummy path U[0.9,1.1)).

Existing coverage: commitpath_unit_test.go:349-392 tests jitterBackoff but ENSHRINES the ±10% law (asserts the result lands within [0.9*base,1.1*base]); it does not assert C++ alignment, so it does not pin/refute this divergence. No differential test in pkg/fdbgo/bench covers the dummy jitter.

Impact: this is an internal commit_unknown_result synchronization-barrier timing detail with no wire effect, no data effect, no correctness effect — hence low severity. But the cited concrete behavioral difference (U[0.9,1.1) vs U[0,1) per-attempt jitter) is real and proven, so the verdict is CONFIRMED.

surviving.title: Iterator() with StreamingModeExact and no row limit returns all rows instead of exact_mode_without_limits (2210)
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/fdb/range_result.go:144 (StreamingModeExact returns full `remaining` with no limit/target validation); the missing validation should sit at the GetRange/Iterator/GetSliceWithError entry — range_result.go:119-132 (Iterator) and range_result.go:91-106 (GetSliceWithError, which ignores Mode entirely), mirroring libfdb_c's validate_and_update_parameters called at fdb_transaction_get_range time.
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:996-998 (validate_and_update_parameters returns exact_mode_without_limits for EXACT mode with limit==ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED && target_bytes==BYTE_LIMIT_UNLIMITED); called from fdb_transaction_get_range_impl at fdb_c.cpp:1049-1051; error code at /tmp/fdbsrc/flow/include/flow/error_definitions.h:303 (exact_mode_without_limits, 2210).
surviving.description: With RangeOptions{Mode: StreamingModeExact} and Limit=0 (no limit), the pure-Go Iterator() computes remaining=effectiveLimit(0)=MaxInt32, batchSize(Exact,...) returns remaining (range_result.go:144), and it fetches the whole range with no error. libfdb_c's validate_and_update_parameters rejects EXACT streaming mode when neither a row limit nor a byte target is set: when limit==ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED && target_bytes==BYTE_LIMIT_UNLIMITED && mode==FDB_STREAMING_MODE_EXACT it returns exact_mode_without_limits (error 2210). The Apple Go binding's Iterator forwards options.Mode unchanged (only GetSlice overrides the mode), so cgofdb's iterator surfaces 2210, while the pure-Go iterator returns data. GetSliceWithError is unaffected (it ignores Mode), so this only manifests on the explicit-Exact Iterator path, which is the documented sub-shape differential_read_test.go does NOT cover (it tests Exact only WITH a limit).
surviving.whyWrong: fdb_c.cpp:996-998 returns exact_mode_without_limits for EXACT mode with no limit/target_bytes; pure-Go returns all rows with no error.
surviving.repro: Seed a few keys under a prefix; pin both clients to the same read version. Then for the pure-Go client: `it := goTx.GetRange(goPrefixRange, gofdb.RangeOptions{Mode: gofdb.StreamingModeExact}).Iterator(); it.Advance(); _, goErr := it.Get()` → goErr==nil and rows are returned. For the cgo client: `it := cTx.GetRange(cPrefixRange, cgofdb.RangeOptions{Mode: cgofdb.StreamingModeExact}).Iterator(); it.Advance(); _, cErr := it.Get()` → cErr is an FDB error with code 2210 (exact_mode_without_limits). Differential assertion: error codes must match; Go yields nil while cgo yields 2210 → mismatch. Equivalent/broader: call `.GetSliceWithError()` on both with `RangeOptions{Mode: StreamingModeExact}` (Limit unset = 0) — pure-Go returns (rows, nil); cgo returns (nil, 2210). Note: the existing goRangeAt/cgoRangeAt harness (differential_read_test.go:101-121) must be extended to thread a Go-side Mode into the options, since today it never sets Mode on the Go side.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
I read the actual code on both sides.

PURE-GO (range_result.go): effectiveLimit(0)=math.MaxInt32 (lines 64-69). Iterator() builds goRangeIterator{remaining: effectiveLimit(rr.options.Limit)} (lines 119-132). Advance() (lines 195-266) calls batchSize(ri.rr.options.Mode, 1, MaxInt32); for StreamingModeExact, batchSize returns `remaining` (lines 144-145) = MaxInt32, then doRangeWithLimit fetches the whole range with no error. GetSliceWithError() (lines 91-106) never references rr.options.Mode at all — it uses only effectiveLimit — so it too returns all rows. There is NO code path in the pure-Go read path that produces error 2210 (fdb/error.go:320 merely maps the numeric code to the name "exact_mode_without_limits" for display; nothing returns it). RangeOptions has a Mode field (range.go:120-137) and StreamingModeExact=1 (range.go:101); Transaction.GetRange (transaction.go:99-101) does no validation.

APPLE BINDING / libfdb_c (the spec, the differential's "B" side): transaction.go:295 getRange() eagerly issues the first request via doGetRange(r, options, false, 1) (transaction.go:268-293), passing C.int(options.Limit)=0, target_bytes C.int(0), C.FDBStreamingMode(options.Mode-1)=0=FDB_STREAMING_MODE_EXACT, iteration=1. That enters fdb_transaction_get_range_impl (fdb_c.cpp:1034-1061) → validate_and_update_parameters (fdb_c.cpp:977-1032): limit 0→ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED (line 990-991), target_bytes 0→BYTE_LIMIT_UNLIMITED (line 992-993), then lines 996-998 `if (limit==ROW_LIMIT_UNLIMITED && target_bytes==BYTE_LIMIT_UNLIMITED && mode==FDB_STREAMING_MODE_EXACT) return TSAV_ERROR(..., exact_mode_without_limits);`. error_definitions.h:303 defines exact_mode_without_limits=2210. So rr.f is an error future carrying 2210, surfaced on the first Advance()/Get() (range.go:212-230, 259-264).

Concrete divergence on input `tx.GetRange(r, RangeOptions{Mode: StreamingModeExact}) /* Limit=0 */`: pure-Go returns all rows with nil error on both Iterator() and GetSliceWithError(); libfdb_c (Apple binding) returns error 2210 on the first Advance/Get for both paths. The StreamingMode value mapping lines up (Apple generated.go:736 StreamingModeExact=1, doGetRange subtracts 1 → C EXACT=0).

CORRECTION to the finding's scope: it claims cgo GetSliceWithError is "unaffected (it ignores Mode)" — inaccurate. The Apple binding builds the FIRST future at GetRange construction time with the original options.Mode=Exact (transaction.go:296), so cgo's GetSliceWithError ALSO returns 2210 (the mode override at range.go:140-144 only retunes subsequent fetchNextBatch calls, not the already-issued first future). Thus the divergence manifests on BOTH the Iterator and GetSliceWithError paths whenever the caller sets Mode=Exact with no limit — broader than the finding stated, and still entirely uncovered (differential_read_test.go:177 tests Exact only with limit=20, and the Go-side harness never sets Mode).

Severity low: this is a misuse-detection error (the caller violated the API contract by requesting Exact streaming without a row/byte limit); the practical consequence is the Go client silently performs an unbounded fetch instead of erroring. Not data corruption and not wire-format incompatibility, but a genuine behavioral divergence from the libfdb_c spec.

surviving.title: Versionstamp offset-suffix transform for API version < 520 is unimplemented — wire divergence vs libfdb_c
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1319-1326 (Atomic's only version gate is apiVersionAtLeast(510); no <520 withSuffix branch), with the unconditional 4-byte offset reads at transaction.go:1391 (versionstampKeyRange) and transaction.go:2220 (validateVersionstampOffset, called from the commit path at 1585/1590). API floor at fdb/database.go:32 (>=510, so 510-519 reachable); Go's own <520 producer at fdb/tuple/tuple.go:634-640. Facade entries fdb/transaction.go:229-236.
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2251-2255 (SetVersionstampedKey withSuffix "\x00\x00" when !apiVersionAtLeast(520)) and :2257-2261 (SetVersionstampedValue withSuffix "\x00\x00\x00\x00"); offset parse at /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/include/fdbclient/Atomic.h:258-264 (parseVersionstampOffset, always last 4 bytes); value-offset validation after the append at ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2279-2287.
surviving.description: libfdb_c makes the versionstamp offset-suffix encoding API-version dependent. For apiVersionAtLeast(520)==false it rewrites the operands BEFORE offset parsing: SetVersionstampedKey -> k = key.withSuffix("\x00\x00") (the user supplies a 2-byte LE offset at the key tail; the 2 appended zero bytes promote it to the 4-byte LE format), and SetVersionstampedValue -> v = operand.withSuffix("\x00\x00\x00\x00") (the <520 value carries NO offset; the appended 4 zero bytes force offset 0 so the stamp lands at byte 0 of the value). The Go Atomic() implements only the adjacent apiVersionAtLeast(510) Min->MinV2/And->AndV2 upgrade (transaction.go:1319) and never branches on 520: versionstampKeyRange (:1391) and validateVersionstampOffset (:2220) always read key[len-4:]/value[len-4:] as a 4-byte LE offset. So for a DB opened with WithAPIVersion(v), 13<=v<520, the same user input that libfdb_c accepts and commits is, in Go, either rejected with client_invalid_operation(2000) or written with a different offset / different number of trailing bytes stripped — a wire-compat divergence on data a C/Java app at the same API version reads.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2251-2255: `if (!tr.apiVersionAtLeast(520) && op==SetVersionstampedKey) k = key.withSuffix("\x00\x00"); ` and :2257-2261 for the value with `"\x00\x00\x00\x00"`. Then Atomic.h:258 parseVersionstampOffset always reads the LAST 4 bytes as int32 LE. Go has no such withSuffix step (transaction.go:1313-1378), so it parses the user's raw tail as a 4-byte offset. Concrete: at API 519, SetVersionstampedKey with key = "ABC"+<10-byte placeholder>+LE16(3) (15 bytes, valid <520 form). libfdb_c appends \x00\x00 -> 17 bytes, parses offset 3, begin=13 bytes, 3+10<=13 OK, commits "ABC"+stamp. Go reads LE32 of the last 4 bytes (= last 2 placeholder bytes ++ LE16(3)) = a large/garbage offset, fails validateVersionstampOffset -> returns FDBError 2000, rejecting a mutation libfdb_c commits. Symmetrically SetVersionstampedValue at API<520 with operand="hello"+? : libfdb_c places the stamp at byte 0 of the value; Go interprets the operand tail as an offset and diverges (rejects or mis-places).
surviving.repro: Open two databases at API version 510: Go via client.OpenDatabase(..., WithAPIVersion(510)) and libfdb_c via fdb_select_api_version_impl(510, 730). In each, run one txn doing SetVersionstampedValue(key, operand) where operand is the <520 value form = a 10-byte all-zero placeholder with NO 4-byte offset suffix (len(operand)==10). Commit. EXPECT: libfdb_c commit returns code 0 and stores a 10-byte materialized versionstamp at byte 0 of the value; Go's Commit returns FDBError code 2000 (client_invalid_operation) and errors the txn — a divergence (one commits, the other rejects). Companion case: SetVersionstampedKey(key) where key = isoPrefix + 10-byte placeholder + LE16(offset) (2-byte <520 offset tail). libfdb_c appends \x00\x00 and stamps at `offset`; Go reads the last 4 bytes (2 placeholder bytes ++ the 2-byte offset) as a bogus 4-byte offset and rejects or mis-places. Both shapes are also producible end-to-end via Go's own tuple.PackWithVersionstamp at API < 520 (it emits the 2-byte key offset), proving the bug without any C client. None of the existing differential_versionstamp_test.go cases set API < 520, so this dimension is currently unpinned.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
I read both sides in full.

GO (no <520 handling): client/transaction.go:1313 Atomic() has exactly one version gate — apiVersionAtLeast(510) for Min→MinV2/And→AndV2 (lines 1319-1326). There is no apiVersionAtLeast(520) branch and no withSuffix step anywhere; grep "520" over client/transaction.go returns nothing. versionstampKeyRange (line 1391) does `pos := int32(binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(key[len(key)-4:]))` unconditionally, and validateVersionstampOffset (line 2220) does `offset := int32(binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(data[len(data)-4:]))` unconditionally; it is invoked from the commit path at lines 1585 (Key) and 1590 (Value). So Go ALWAYS treats the trailing 4 bytes as the offset, regardless of API version.

C++ (version-dependent suffix transform): ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2251-2255 — `if (!tr.apiVersionAtLeast(520) && operationType == MutationRef::SetVersionstampedKey) k = key.withSuffix("\x00\x00"_sr, arena);` — and :2257-2261 — `if (!tr.apiVersionAtLeast(520) && operationType == MutationRef::SetVersionstampedValue) v = operand.withSuffix("\x00\x00\x00\x00"_sr, arena);`. Then parseVersionstampOffset (Atomic.h:258-264) reads the last 4 bytes, and the value path validates at :2279-2287 AFTER the append. So at API < 520, libfdb_c expects a 2-byte offset on the KEY and NO offset on the VALUE.

REACHABILITY: fdb/database.go:32 APIVersion() rejects only version < 510 (returns 2201), so 510-519 are valid; client.OpenDatabase accepts any non-zero WithAPIVersion (database.go:726). libfdb_c 7.3.77 accepts these legacy versions too. (Finding's "13<=v<520" overstates the floor — Go's actual floor is 510 — but the <520 range is still non-empty.) STRONGER: Go's OWN tuple layer fdb/tuple/tuple.go:634-640 emits a 2-byte LE offset (offsetIndex=2) when apiVersion < 520, i.e. Go produces the <520 key form that Go's Atomic/commit path then misreads as a 4-byte offset — an internal self-inconsistency, independent of any cross-client comparison.

CONCRETE DIVERGENCE (cleanest): API 510, SetVersionstampedValue with operand = 10-byte placeholder (the <520 value form: stamp at pos 0, NO 4-byte offset suffix). libfdb_c appends \x00\x00\x00\x00 → v.size()=14, offset=0, 0+10 <= 14-4=10 → COMMITS (code 0), stamp at byte 0. Go: validateVersionstampOffset(len=10): offset=LE32(operand[6:10]), bodyLen=6, offset+10 > 6 for any offset≥0 → returns FDBError{2000} client_invalid_operation and errors the txn. libfdb_c commits, Go rejects. Symmetric mis-handling for SetVersionstampedKey with a 2-byte offset tail (Go reads 2 data/placeholder bytes ++ the 2-byte offset as a garbage 4-byte offset).

COVERAGE: every case in bench/differential_versionstamp_test.go uses the default API (>=520, 4-byte suffix; see its header comment and vsOperand "4-byte LE offset suffix (API >= 520)"). The <520 dimension is unpinned, so no existing differential refutes this.

I downgrade severity medium→low: the divergence is genuine and self-consistently reproducible, but reachable only at legacy API versions 510-519, while this codebase targets 730; real-world blast radius is small.

surviving.title: Missing legacy apiVersion<520 SetVersionstampedKey/Value operand suffix-append
surviving.goLocation: pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1319 (Atomic: the apiVersionAtLeast(510) block is the spot that should also carry the <520 SVK/SVV suffix-append; validateVersionstampOffset at transaction.go:2216 and the commit-loop call sites at :1585/:1590 also lack apiVersion awareness)
surviving.cppReference: /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2250-2261 (suffix append for api<520) and :2279-2287 (SVV offset validation on the suffixed operand); supported-version floor at /tmp/fdbsrc/bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1408-1409
surviving.description: For apiVersion<520, C++ atomicOp rewrites the operand before validation/commit: SetVersionstampedKey appends a 2-byte zero suffix to the key (`k = key.withSuffix("\x00\x00")`, ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2251-2255) and SetVersionstampedValue appends a 4-byte zero suffix to the value (`v = operand.withSuffix("\x00\x00\x00\x00")`, :2257-2261) — the legacy offset encoding where the offset field was not supplied by the user. The SVV offset validation at :2280-2287 then runs on the suffixed value (pos read from the appended zero bytes = 0). Go's Atomic (transaction.go:1313-1378) implements only the >=510 Min/And upgrade and never appends these legacy suffixes; validateVersionstampOffset (transaction.go:2216) unconditionally parses the user's last 4 bytes as the offset. So at apiVersion<520 the committed wire operand differs and the accept/reject decision flips.
surviving.whyWrong: C++ appends \x00\x00 (key) / \x00\x00\x00\x00 (value) at apiVersion<520 (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2251-2261); Go appends nothing and validates the raw user operand, producing different wire bytes and rejecting operands libfdb_c accepts.
surviving.repro: Differential test, both clients at apiVersion 500 (Go: WithAPIVersion(500); C: fdb_select_api_version(500)). In a transaction, call SetVersionstampedValue(key=[]byte("k"), param=[]byte("0123456789")) [10-byte operand, NO offset suffix] and commit. Expected libfdb_c: commit SUCCEEDS — it appends "\x00\x00\x00\x00" so v=14 bytes, offset=0, stamp written at value offset 0 (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2257-2287). Observed Go: commit FAILS with FDBError 2000 (client_invalid_operation) because validateVersionstampOffset parses "6789" as the offset (transaction.go:2216-2224). Assert the two clients agree on commit success/failure AND, on success, on the stored value bytes after the stamp is applied. A secondary shape (both accept, bytes differ): operand=[]byte("value\x00\x00\x00\x00") (9 bytes) at api 500 — libfdb_c appends another 4 zeros (stamp at offset 0, value 9 bytes after strip), Go reads offset=0 from the user's last 4 bytes and places the stamp at offset 0 of a 5-byte value => different stored bytes / different validation outcome.
surviving.verifierReasoning:
All four premises independently verified by reading the actual code on both sides.

C++ (read /tmp/fdbsrc/fdbclient/ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2250-2261): in atomicOp(), for `!tr.apiVersionAtLeast(520)`, SVK gets `k = key.withSuffix("\x00\x00", arena)` (2-byte append, :2251-2252) and SVV gets `v = operand.withSuffix("\x00\x00\x00\x00", arena)` (4-byte append, :2257-2258). The SVV validation at :2279-2287 then runs on the SUFFIXED v: it memcpys `pos` from `v.end()-4` (the appended zero bytes => pos=0) and checks `pos + 10 > v.size() - 4`. For a 10-byte operand, v.size()=14, so `0 + 10 > 10` is false => ACCEPTED, committing a 14-byte operand with the stamp at offset 0.

C++ (read /tmp/fdbsrc/bindings/c/fdb_c.cpp:1395-1410): fdb_select_api_version_impl accepts any runtime_version in [13, FDB_API_VERSION]. So apiVersion 500 (<520) is a fully supported libfdb_c 7.3.77 configuration.

Go (read pkg/fdbgo/client/transaction.go:1313-1378, Atomic): implements ONLY the apiVersionAtLeast(510) Min->MinV2 / And->AndV2 upgrade (:1319-1326). There is no `<520` SetVersionstampedKey/Value suffix-append anywhere. The SVK-specific branch (:1341-1376) handles conflict ranges / RYW unreadable ranges but never appends `\x00\x00`; SVV has no special handling at all.

Go (read transaction.go:1503-1594 commit loop + 2216-2226 validateVersionstampOffset): the commit loop iterates tx.mutations unconditionally (independent of RYW) and at :1589-1593 calls validateVersionstampOffset(m.Value) for SVV. That function (:2216) parses `offset := int32(LE(data[len-4:]))` from the RAW user operand with no apiVersion awareness. For operand "0123456789" the last 4 bytes "6789" decode to a large positive offset; `offset+10 > 6` => returns FDBError 2000 (client_invalid_operation) => REJECTED.

Go (read database.go:726 + options.go:122): OpenDatabaseFromConfig rejects only apiVersion==0; 500 is accepted and the client operates. No other apiVersion floor exists.

Concrete divergence on input apiVersion=500, SetVersionstampedValue(key, []byte("0123456789")): libfdb_c ACCEPTS and commits a 14-byte operand (stamp at offset 0); Go REJECTS with client_invalid_operation (2000). The accept/reject decision flips AND the committed wire bytes differ (Go would also differ for any well-formed >=520-style operand at api<520, placing the stamp at the user offset instead of offset 0). The finder's cited Go location (transaction.go:1313, Atomic — where the suffix logic should live) and C++ location (ReadYourWrites.actor.cpp:2251) are both correct.

Existing coverage check: differential_tuple_test.go:370-372 declares the "API<520 2-byte branch out of differential scope" but that is the gotuple.PackWithVersionstamp tuple-packing layer, a distinct code path from the atomicOp/RYW suffix-append. apiversion_atomic_test.go pins only the Min/And upgrade gate (it constructs apiVersion:500 but never exercises versionstamp suffix behavior). No test pins the atomicOp-level <520 suffix-append, so this is genuinely untested. This is NOT in the KNOWN-RESOLVED list (RFC-149 covers only Min/And; RFC-056/057 cover getKey; etc.). Severity low because pre-520 is a legacy API rarely used in practice, but it is a real, supported-by-libfdb_c, client-side wire/accept-reject divergence.
